court: Supreme Court of the United States
text: MARBURY v MADISON (1803). COURT: Supreme Court of the United States. FACTS: William Marbury was appointed as a Justice of the Peace by President John Adams in the final days of his administration. The commission was signed but never delivered. Incoming Secretary of State James Madison refused to deliver the commission. Marbury petitioned the Supreme Court for a writ of mandamus to compel delivery. LEGAL ISSUES: (1) Whether Marbury had a right to the commission. (2) Whether the laws of the country afforded him a remedy. (3) Whether the Supreme Court had original jurisdiction to issue the writ. HOLDING: Chief Justice John Marshall held that while Marbury had a right to his commission, the Supreme Court lacked original jurisdiction to issue the writ because Section 13 of the Judiciary Act of 1789 was unconstitutional insofar as it purported to extend the Court's original jurisdiction beyond what the Constitution permitted. REASONING: Marshall established the principle of JUDICIAL REVIEW — the power of the courts to review legislative acts and declare them void if they are repugnant to the Constitution. He wrote: 'It is emphatically the province and duty of the Judicial Department to say what the law is.' The Constitution is the supreme law of the land, and any legislative act that is repugnant to the Constitution is void. SIGNIFICANCE: (1) Established JUDICIAL REVIEW — the most important case in American constitutional law. (2) Established the Supreme Court as the ultimate arbiter of the Constitution. (3) Foundation of constitutional supremacy.

case_name: Brown v Board of Education of Topeka
court: Supreme Court of the United States
citation: Brown v Board of Education of Topeka, 347 U.S. 483 (1954)
text: BROWN v BOARD OF EDUCATION (1954). COURT: Supreme Court of the United States. FACTS: African American children were denied admission to public schools attended by white children under laws requiring or permitting segregation by race. The cases arose from Kansas, South Carolina, Virginia, and Delaware. Plaintiffs argued that segregated schools were inherently unequal and violated the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment. LEGAL ISSUES: Whether separate but equal educational facilities for racial minorities violated the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment. HOLDING: Chief Justice Earl Warren, writing for a unanimous Court, held that 'separate educational facilities are inherently unequal' and therefore violate the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment. REASONING: The Court examined the effect of segregation on public education and found that separating children solely on the basis of race generates a feeling of inferiority that may affect their hearts and minds in a way unlikely ever to be undone. Warren wrote: 'To separate them from others of similar age and qualifications solely because of their race generates a feeling of inferiority as to their status in the community that may affect their hearts and minds in a way unlikely ever to be undone.' The Court explicitly overruled Plessy v Ferguson (1896), which had established the 'separate but equal' doctrine. SIGNIFICANCE: (1) Overruled the SEPARATE BUT EQUAL doctrine. (2) Landmark in civil rights jurisprudence. (3) Catalyzed the American civil rights movement. (4) Established that state-sponsored racial segregation violates the Equal Protection Clause.

court: Supreme Court of the United States
text: MIRANDA v ARIZONA (1966). COURT: Supreme Court of the United States. FACTS: Ernesto Miranda was arrested and interrogated by police without being informed of his right to an attorney or his right against self-incrimination. He confessed to kidnapping and rape during the interrogation. LEGAL ISSUES: Whether statements obtained from an individual during custodial police interrogation are admissible when the individual has not been informed of their Fifth Amendment rights. HOLDING: Chief Justice Earl Warren held that the prosecution may not use statements stemming from custodial interrogation unless it demonstrates the use of procedural safeguards effective to secure the privilege against self-incrimination. Prior to questioning, the person must be warned of the right to remain silent, that any statement may be used against them, and the right to an attorney. REASONING: The Court found that the inherently coercive nature of custodial interrogation required procedural safeguards. The Fifth Amendment privilege against self-incrimination is available outside of criminal court proceedings and protects persons in all settings from being compelled to incriminate themselves. SIGNIFICANCE: Established MIRANDA RIGHTS — the requirement that individuals be informed of their rights before custodial interrogation. One of the most well-known and consequential criminal procedure decisions in American law.

court: Supreme Court of the United States
text: ROE v WADE (1973). COURT: Supreme Court of the United States. FACTS: Jane Roe challenged a Texas law that criminalized abortion except to save the life of the mother. LEGAL ISSUES: Whether the Constitution recognizes a woman's right to terminate her pregnancy. HOLDING: Justice Blackmun held that the right to privacy under the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment extended to a woman's decision to have an abortion, but that this right must be balanced against the state's interests in protecting prenatal life and the health of the mother. REASONING: The Court established a trimester framework: in the first trimester, the decision is left to the woman and her physician; in the second trimester, the state may regulate abortion to protect maternal health; in the third trimester, the state may prohibit abortion except when necessary to preserve the life or health of the mother. SIGNIFICANCE: Established the constitutional right to abortion (later modified by Planned Parenthood v Casey (1992) and overruled by Dobbs v Jackson Women's Health Organization (2022)). One of the most controversial and consequential decisions in American constitutional law.

case_name: New York Times Co. v Sullivan
court: Supreme Court of the United States
citation: New York Times Co. v Sullivan, 376 U.S. 254 (1964)
text: NEW YORK TIMES v SULLIVAN (1964). COURT: Supreme Court of the United States. FACTS: L.B. Sullivan, a Montgomery, Alabama city commissioner, sued the New York Times for defamation based on a full-page advertisement that contained some factual inaccuracies about police actions during the civil rights movement. LEGAL ISSUES: Whether the First and Fourteenth Amendments limit the power of a state to award damages in a libel action brought by a public official. HOLDING: Justice Brennan held that the First Amendment requires a public official suing for defamation to prove that the statement was made with ACTUAL MALICE — that is, with knowledge that it was false or with reckless disregard for whether it was true or false. REASONING: The Court emphasized the 'profound national commitment to the principle that debate on public issues should be uninhibited, robust, and wide-open.' Erroneous statements are inevitable in free debate and must be protected to provide the 'breathing space' that freedoms of expression require. SIGNIFICANCE: (1) Established the ACTUAL MALICE standard for public officials in defamation cases. (2) Landmark First Amendment case protecting press freedom. (3) Foundational for freedom of expression jurisprudence.

court: Supreme Court of the United States
text: GIDEON v WAINWRIGHT (1963). COURT: Supreme Court of the United States. FACTS: Clarence Earl Gideon was charged with a felony in Florida state court. He requested that the court appoint an attorney for him because he could not afford one. The court refused, as Florida law only required appointment of counsel for capital offenses. Gideon represented himself and was convicted. HOLDING: Justice Black held unanimously that the Sixth Amendment right to counsel is incorporated against the states through the Fourteenth Amendment. States are required to provide counsel in criminal cases for defendants who cannot afford an attorney. SIGNIFICANCE: Established the constitutional right to appointed counsel for indigent defendants in all criminal cases, not just capital cases. Foundation of the public defender system.

case_name: Citizens United v Federal Election Commission
court: Supreme Court of the United States
citation: Citizens United v Federal Election Commission, 558 U.S. 310 (2010)
text: CITIZENS UNITED v FEC (2010). COURT: Supreme Court of the United States. FACTS: Citizens United, a nonprofit corporation, sought to air and advertise a film critical of Hillary Clinton during the 2008 primary elections. Federal law prohibited corporations from using general treasury funds for electioneering communications within 30 days of a primary or 60 days of a general election. HOLDING: Justice Kennedy held that the First Amendment prohibits the government from restricting independent political expenditures by corporations, associations, or labor unions. REASONING: Political speech does not lose First Amendment protection simply because its source is a corporation rather than an individual. The Court overruled Austin v Michigan Chamber of Commerce (1990), which had upheld restrictions on corporate political spending. SIGNIFICANCE: (1) Established that corporations have First Amendment rights to political speech. (2) Led to the rise of Super PACs in American politics. (3) One of the most consequential and controversial campaign finance decisions.

court: Supreme Court of the United States
text: OBERGEFELL v HODGES (2015). COURT: Supreme Court of the United States. FACTS: Same-sex couples in Ohio, Michigan, Kentucky, and Tennessee challenged state laws that defined marriage as between one man and one woman, and refused to recognize same-sex marriages performed in other jurisdictions. HOLDING: Justice Kennedy held that the Fourteenth Amendment requires all states to grant and recognize same-sex marriages. The fundamental right to marry is guaranteed to same-sex couples under the Due Process and Equal Protection Clauses. REASONING: Four principles and traditions demonstrate that marriage is a fundamental right: (1) the right to personal choice regarding marriage is inherent in individual autonomy; (2) marriage supports a two-person union unlike any other; (3) marriage safeguards children and families; and (4) marriage is a keystone of social order. SIGNIFICANCE: Established the constitutional right to same-sex marriage nationwide. Landmark in LGBTQ+ rights and equal protection jurisprudence.
